attributes.stem:
A 73-year-old man is referred to the Urology clinic with a one-year history of worsening urinary symptoms. He gets up three to four times each night to urinate, has a weak urinary stream, hesitancy, and a sensation of incomplete voiding. He does not have any other urinary symptoms. His medical history includes hypertension and osteoarthritis of his knees, for which he takes perindopril and diclofenac. He drinks a glass of wine most evenings.

On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Abdominal examination reveals no tenderness or palpable bladder. Digital rectal examination reveals a smooth, symmetrically enlarged prostate with no nodules. Urinalysis is negative for blood, leukocytes, and nitrites. His prostate-specific antigen level is 3.2 μg/L (0-4.0). Ultrasound shows an enlarged prostate of 55 mL with noticeable middle lobe enlargement and a post-void residual volume of 85 mL.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate plan of management?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with moderate to severe lower urinary tract symptoms (LUTS) consistent with benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH). His symptoms include nocturia, weak stream, hesitancy, and incomplete voiding. The ultrasound findings of an enlarged prostate (55 mL) with middle lobe enlargement and a significant post-void residual volume (85 mL) are important factors in determining management.

Tamsulosin is an alpha-1 adrenergic receptor antagonist that relaxes smooth muscle in the prostate and bladder neck, improving urinary flow. This patient should be given a trial of tamsulosin. While it can be effective for mild to moderate BPH symptoms, it is less likely to provide adequate relief in this case given the significant middle lobe enlargement and elevated post-void residual volume. Alpha blockers generally work best for functional rather than mechanical obstruction.

Transurethral resection of the prostate (TURP) would be considered if the alpha blocker was not helpful. TURP is better for men with a prominent middle lobe, however some respond favourably with medication alone. TURP as a first line option (without trying medication) is used in men with evidence of long standing outflow obstruction including urinary retention, large residuals (often > 400 mL), bladder stones or hydronephrosis.

Dutasteride is a 5-alpha reductase inhibitor that reduces prostate size by inhibiting the conversion of testosterone to dihydrotestosterone. It typically takes 3-6 months to show clinical benefit and is most effective for larger prostates (>80 cc). However, it is less effective for middle lobe enlargement and would not address this patient's symptoms quickly enough given their severity and duration. Dutasteride lowers the active testosterone in men and can result in low libido and gynaecomastia. It is best to avoid these agents with such side effects in men who are unlikely to gain a clinical benefit.

Tamsulosin and dutasteride combination therapy can be more effective than either medication alone for moderate to severe BPH. However, given this patient's significant middle lobe enlargement and elevated post-void residual volume, medical therapy is less likely to provide adequate symptom relief compared to surgical intervention. However, a trial period with tamsulosin would be appropriate.

Laser ablation techniques such as holmium laser enucleation of the prostate (HoLEP) or photoselective vaporisation of the prostate (PVP) are valid alternatives to TURP. While these procedures may offer advantages such as reduced bleeding risk and shorter catheteris, ation time, TURP remains the reference standard for prostates of this size. Laser ablation would be particularly advantageous in patients on anticoagulation therapy, which is not mentioned in this case.
learningPoints: Benign prostatic hyperplasia with significant middle lobe enlargement often responds better to surgical intervention than medical therapy, however a trial with tamsulosin would be appropriate..
Post-void residual volume >80 mL with moderate to severe lower urinary tract symptoms is an indication for surgical management.
Transurethral resection of the prostate remains the gold standard surgical treatment for moderate-sized prostates (30-80 mL).
references: Sandhu JS, Bixler BR, Dahm P, et al. Management of Lower Urinary Tract Symptoms Attributed to Benign Prostatic Hyperplasia (BPH): AUA Guideline Amendment 2023. J Urol. 2024; 211:11-19.

Lerner LB, McVary KT, Barry MJ, et al. Management of Lower Urinary Tract Symptoms Attributed to Benign Prostatic Hyperplasia: AUA GUIDELINE PART I-Initial Work-up and Medical Management. J Urol 2021; 206:806-17.
Mobley D, Feibus A, Baum N. Benign prostatic hyperplasia and urinary symptoms: Evaluation and treatment. Postgraduate Medicine, 127:3, 301-307.
Gharbieh S, Reeves F, Challacombe B. The prostatic middle lobe: clinical significance, presentation and management. Nat Rev Urol. 2023; 20:645-653.
